<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673791 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00471: Exporting images of model diagrams

- page_id: `169673791`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673791/Exporting+images+of+model+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-10-20T13:51:43.121+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > CATIA applications > CATIA FLXML Exporter
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The CATIA FLXML plugin allows you to export the images of elements if the CustomImageHolder has been set to an element transformed to an FL reference in the **3D**EXPERIENCEplatform. For example, the diagram displayed below can be exported as the following logical view.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of exporting the image of a model diagram.

To export images of model elements

1. In the main menu of a modeling tool, go to Options > Environment .
2. On the left side of the Environment Options dialog, select CATIA FLXML Exporter option group.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The CATIA FLXML plugin allows you to export the images of elements if the CustomImageHolder has been set to an element transformed to an FL reference in the <span style="color: rgb(55,65,81);"><strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE </span>platform. For example, the diagram displayed below can be exported as the following logical view.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_export_example.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting images of model diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An example of exporting the image of a model diagram.</h6><p>To export images of model elements</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu of a modeling tool, go to <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Environment</strong>.</li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Environment Options</strong> dialog, select <strong>CATIA FLXML Exporter</strong> option group.</li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673668 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00472: Exporting packages to new projects

- page_id: `169673668`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673668/Exporting+packages+to+new+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:27:56.470+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Project partitioning > Decomposing model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.

Using the **Export Packages to New Project** dialog, you can partition the model and save the content of a selected package as a separate project. Once exported, the package and its elements are read-only, and the project name is displayed in brackets next to the package name in the Model Browser.

To export a package as a new project

1. Do one of the following: The **Export Packages to New Project** dialog opens (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - From the **File** menu, select **Export To** > **Project Usage**.
  - In the Model Browser, select one or more packages you want to save as a separate project. Right-click it (them) and in the shortcut menu select **Project Usages** > **Export Packages to****New Project**.
2. In the **Tree/List** select the package you want to save as a separate project and click the **Add**button. The package is added to the **Selected packages** list.
3. If needed, type a description of the exported packages in the **Project Description** box. This description is displayed in the documentation of the package.
4. Click **OK**.

Before exporting selected packages to a separate project, the modeling tool checks for dependencies between the model and the exported part of the model. You must resolve any errors that occur, otherwise recovered elements may appear in the new project. The dependency resolution process is the same as for resolution of dependencies between shared and not shared parts of the project.

When dependencies are resolved, the modeling tool asks to specify the new project location and name.

This action consist of 3 elementary steps:

- Saving model elements into a separate file.
- Sharing the entire contents of this project.
- Using the project in the main project.

Alternatively, if you have several small, related projects, you can join them into a larger, partitioned project to work with all the information in one place. This can be done by using the **Use Project** button on the **Used Projects** dialog (to open the dialog, select **Options** > **Project Usages**).

Only packages can be exported to new projects. To export an element or diagram, you must move it to a package and then export the package.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6a2670d8-4414-46b4-bb0a-10ad60ff5281"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Using the <strong>Export Packages to New Project</strong> dialog, you can partition the model and save the content of a selected package as a separate project. Once exported, the package and its elements are read-only, and the project name is displayed in brackets next to the package name in the Model Browser.</p><p>To export a package as a new project</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<br /><ul><li><span>From the <strong>File</strong> menu, select <strong>Export To</strong> &gt; <strong>Project Usage</strong>.</span></li><li><span>In the Model Browser, select one or more packages you want to save as a separate </span><span>project. Right-click it (them) and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Export Packages to </strong><span><strong>New Project</strong>.</span></span></li></ul><p>The <strong>Export Packages to New Project</strong> dialog opens (see the following figure).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="export_packages_to_new_project_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting packages to new projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p>In the <strong>Tree/List</strong> select the package you want to save as a separate project and click the <strong>Add </strong>button. The package is added to the <strong>Selected packages</strong> list.</p></li><li><p>If needed, type a description of the exported packages in the <strong>Project Description</strong> box. This description is displayed in the documentation of the package.</p></li><li><p>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li></ol><p>Before exporting selected packages to a separate project, the modeling tool checks for dependencies between the model and the exported part of the model. You must resolve any errors that occur, otherwise recovered elements may appear in the new project. The dependency resolution process is the same as for resolution of dependencies between shared and not shared parts of the project.</p><p>When dependencies are resolved, the modeling tool asks to specify the new project location and name.</p><p>This action consist of 3 elementary steps:</p><ul><li>Saving model elements into a separate file.</li><li>Sharing the entire contents of this project.</li><li>Using the project in the main project.</li></ul><p>Alternatively, if you have several small, related projects, you can join them into a larger, partitioned project to work with all the information in one place. This can be done by using the <strong>Use Project</strong> button on the <strong>Used Projects</strong> dialog (to open the dialog, select <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project Usages</strong>).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c3e952fe-8e8d-4bf7-b148-f7afbaee36fb"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Only packages can be exported to new projects. To export an element or diagram, you must move it to a package and then export the package.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673554 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00473: Exporting projects as templates

- page_id: `169673554`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673554/Exporting+projects+as+templates
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T15:42:16.137+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Creating and using project templates
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Exporting a Teamwork Cloud project to a template directly is not possible. Please save the project locally before exporting it.

You can save (or export) the created project as a template and then use the same project for creating new projects.

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

To export a project as a template

1. Open the project you want to export as a template.
2. From the File menu, select Export To > Template . The Export Template dialog opens.
3. Type the name and the description of the template.
4. Click OK . The project is exported as a template.

- According to the template name in the Export Template dialog, the .mdzip file will be created in <modeling tool installation folder>\templates . For example, type the name LibraryTemplate to save file as LibraryTemplate.mdzip .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0078f918-17e1-4da6-9324-46c794b0a705"><ac:rich-text-body>Exporting a Teamwork Cloud project to a template directly is not possible. Please save the project locally before exporting it.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">You can save (or export) the created project as a template and then use the same project for creating new projects.</span> </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="483edbfb-ba87-4ddd-bafb-62f7780b9ea6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To export a project as a template</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the project you want to export as a template.</li><li>From the <strong>File</strong> menu, select <strong>Export</strong> <strong>To</strong> &gt; <strong>Template</strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4883b184-e672-4b7f-8ce1-5ee8567c26dd">.</ac:inline-comment-marker> The <strong>Export Template</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>Type the name and the description of the template.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>. The project is exported as a template. <br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6239531d-a0eb-4912-bf30-1fb4062970d8"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>According to the template name in the <strong>Export Template</strong> dialog, the <em>.mdzip</em> file will be created in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation folder&gt;\templates</em>. For example, type the name <em>LibraryTemplate</em> to save file as <em>LibraryTemplate.mdzip</em>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673748 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00474: Exporting projects to Ecore files

- page_id: `169673748`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673748/Exporting+projects+to+Ecore+files
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T12:28:10.356+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Ecore support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Ecore model exporting is very similar to the EMOF / CMOF model exporting. After the Ecore model is created, you can export either the whole project or selected packages to an .ecore file.

##### Exporting simple projects

To export a project to an Ecore file

1. From the main menu, select **File** > **Export To** > **EMF Ecore File** >**Ecore Whole Model**. The **Select Target File** dialog opens (see the following figure).
2. Type a file name and select a location for the exported model.
3. Click to select or to clear the **Validate** check box.
4. Click the **Export** button when you are finished. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To export selected packages to Ecore file

1. From the main menu, select **File**> **Export To** > **EMF Ecore File** > **Ecore Selection**.
2. In the **Select Packages to Export** dialog, select packages you want to export and click **OK** when you are done. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Perform the procedure **To export a project to an Ecore file** starting from the **step #2**.

##### Exporting projects containing used projects

It is important to notice that any used projects in a project are not exported together with the project on its export to an Ecore file. Only references to these used projects are exported. Therefore the output of the projectexport is an Ecore file (model) containing the direct content of the project and referencing to other Ecore files (used projects) that are used in the project. For referencing to used projects, i.e., other Ecore files, Ecore references are used.

Each used project used by the project must be exported to an Ecore file individually. For this you have to open each used project as a project and then export it to an Ecore file.

It is strongly recommended to read the following paragraph before your very first attempt to export a project that uses used projects. Getting familiar with this information may help you to escape a serious problem that arises because of the nature of Ecore references.

Ecore references, crossing a resource boundary (when the element in one file references the element in another file), *are qualified-name-based, but not id-based* as in case of CMOF, EMOF, or UML. Hence when exporting a project with references to elements in other projects (used projects), the export tool must know the full path of elements in the used project's Ecore file. This information cannot be determined from UML model without additional information. For this the following approach is adopted:

1. **Save the used project after the export.** After a project is exported to an Ecore file, the qualified names (paths) of the shared elements are recorded in special stereotypes («EcoreExportServiceInformation») / tags (ecoreExportPath) of the project's shared packages. The side effect is that the project is modified during the export. To preserve this information for later usage, you need to save the project after the export. 
2. **Export used projects before exporting the main project.** When a project that references to elements in one or more used projects is exported to an Ecore file, the export tool needs to know these elements' paths, saved on the appropriate used project export to Ecore. If this information is missing (e.g., in case the project is being exported before used projects are exported), the export tool tries to guess the correct path of each element in the used project and gives warnings about this. That is why used projects should be exported to Ecore before exporting the main project that uses these used projects.

##### Exported data type mappings

Standard UML data types are exported as standard Ecore data types. The following table shows which Ecore data type corresponds to which UML data type.

| UML data type | Ecore data type |
| --- | --- |
| String | EString |
| Boolean | EBoolean |
| Integer | EInt |
| UnlimitedNatural | EInt |
| Real | EReal |

Standard data types from the MagicDraw profile are exported as Ecore types. The following table shows whichEcore data type corresponds which UML data type.

| Data types in MagicDraw profile | Ecore data type |
| --- | --- |
| boolean | EBoolean |
| byte | EByte |
| char | EChar |
| date | EDate |
| double | EDouble |
| float | EFloat |
| int | EInt |
| real | EReal |
| long | ELong |
| short | EShort |

The void data type is exported as an absence of the type.

References to Ecore model elements (standard datatypes like EInt, metaclasses like EStructuralFeature), defined in the standard Ecore library are exported as standardized Ecore references to Ecore metamodel elements (the resource identifier part of the Href is [*http://www.eclipse.org/emf/2002/Ecore*](http://www.eclipse.org/emf/2002/Ecore)).

##### Ecore elements validation

The modeling tool provides a validation suite for validating a model that is being exported to an Ecore file. This suite contain batches of rules to check exported elements. Warnings about not exported elements (for example, diagrams, behavioral elements, or other) are displayed after the validation process is completed.

The validation process does not preclude the model from being exported. Unsuitable elements are simply skipped.

You can run the Ecore validation on a model (project or used project) export to Ecore. All UML elements that are not suitable for the Ecore, will be highlighted.

You can also run this validation suite at any time while you are developing an Ecore model. 
 
 
To run the Ecore validation

1. From the main menu, select Analyze > Validation > Validate . The Validation dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='ecore_validation.png']
2. In the Validation Suite drop-down list, select Ecore Validation .
3. In the Validate For drop-down list, select the scope of the validation.
4. In the **Minimal Severity** drop-down list, select the level of severity.
5. Click **Validate**.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">The Ecore model exporting is very similar to the EMOF / CMOF model exporting. After the Ecore model is created, you can export either the whole project or selected packages to an .ecore file. </span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Exporting simple projects</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">To export a project to an Ecore file</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p>From the main menu, select <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Export To</strong> &gt; <strong>EMF Ecore File</strong> &gt;<strong> Ecore Whole Model</strong>. The <strong>Select Target File</strong> dialog opens (see the following figure).</p></li><li><p>Type a file name and select a location for the exported model.</p></li><li><p>Click to select or to clear the <strong>Validate</strong> check box.</p></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Export</strong> button when you are finished.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="exportig_whole_model_ecore.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting projects to Ecore files" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><p style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> To export selected packages to Ecore file</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p>From the main menu, select <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Export To</strong> &gt; <strong>EMF Ecore File</strong> &gt; <strong>Ecore Selection</strong>.</p></li><li><p>In the <strong>Select Packages to Export</strong> dialog, select packages you want to export and click <strong>OK</strong> when you are done.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_packages_to_export_ecore.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting projects to Ecore files" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li><p>Perform the procedure <strong>To export a project to an Ecore file</strong> starting from the <strong>step #2</strong>.</p></li></ol><h3 style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> Exporting projects containing used projects</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">It is important to notice that any used projects in a project are not exported together with the project on its export to an Ecore file. Getting familiar with this information may help you to escape a serious problem that arises because of the nature of Ecore references. </span></p><p style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Ecore references, crossing a resource boundary (when the element in one file references the element in another file), <em>are qualified-name-based, but not id-based</em> as in case of CMOF, EMOF, or UML. Hence when exporting a project with references to elements in other projects (used projects), the export tool must know the full path of elements in the used project's Ecore file. This information cannot be determined from UML model without additional information. For this the following approach is adopted:</span></p><p style="color: rgb(129,137,156);margin-left: 30.0px;"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> 1. <strong>Save the used project after the export.</strong> After a project is exported to an Ecore file, the qualified names (paths) of the shared elements are recorded in special stereotypes («EcoreExportServiceInformation») / tags (ecoreExportPath) of the project's shared packages. The side effect is that the project is modified during the export. To preserve this information for later usage, you need to save the project after the export.<br /></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> 2. <strong>Export used projects before exporting the main project.</strong> When a project that references to elements in one or more used projects is exported to an Ecore file, the export tool needs to know these elements' paths, saved on the appropriate used project export to Ecore. If this information is missing (e.g., in case the project is being exported before used projects are exported), the export tool tries to guess the correct path of each element in the used project and gives warnings about this. That is why used projects should be exported to Ecore before exporting the main project that uses these used projects. </span></p><h3 style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Exported data type mappings</span></h3><p style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Standard UML data types are exported as standard Ecore data types. The following table shows which Ecore data type corresponds to which UML data type.</span></p><table><tbody><tr><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>UML data type</strong></span></p></td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Ecore data type</strong></span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">String</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">EString</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Boolean</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">EBoolean</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Integer</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">EInt</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">UnlimitedNatural</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">EInt</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Real</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">EReal</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> Standard data types from the MagicDraw profile are exported as Ecore types. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673727 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00475: Exporting projects to MOF files

- page_id: `169673727`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673727/Exporting+projects+to+MOF+files
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:51:06.099+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > MOF support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The modeling tool allows for exporting a selected project either to EMOF or the CMOF package. You can choose to export either the whole project or selected packages only. The model, all except auxiliary resources (for example, the UML Standard Profile package), will be exported to the selected MOF file.

Diagram data and model features available only in UML (behavioral models in particular) cannot be exported.

To export a project to a MOF file

1. From the main menu, select File > Export To > MOF XMI File > MOF Whole Model . The Select Target File dialog opens (see the following figure).
2. Type the file name and select the location for the exported model.
3. On the right side of the dialog, select MOF Kind . The file type of the exported model changes according to your selection.
4. Click to select or to clear selection from the Validate check box.
5. Click the Export button when you are finished. [ATTACHMENT filename='exporting_whole_model_mof.png']

To export selected packages to a MOF file

1. From the main menu, select **File** > **Export To** > **MOF XMI File** > **MOF Selection**.
2. In the **Select****Packages****to****Export** dialog, select packages you want to export and click **Add** to move them to the **Selected** list. Click **OK** when you are done. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Perform the further steps as described in the previous exportprocedure (starting from **step #2**).

**Exported****elements validation** 
The modeling tool provides two validation suites (one for CMOF and one for EMOF) for validating models that are being exported to MOF files. These suites contain batches of rules to check exported elements.

Warnings about elements that were not exported (for example, diagrams, behavioral elements, or other) are displayed upon completion of the validation process.Validation does not preclude the model from being exported, the ineligible elements are simply skipped.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The<strong> Select Target File</strong> dialog opens (see the following figure).</li><li>Type the file name and select the location for the exported model.</li><li>On the right side of the dialog, select <strong>MOF Kind</strong>. The file type of the exported model changes according to your selection.</li><li>Click to select or to clear selection from the <strong>Validate</strong> check box.</li><li>Click the <strong>Export</strong> button when you are finished.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="exporting_whole_model_mof.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting projects to MOF files" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To export selected packages to a MOF file</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">From the main menu, select <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Export To</strong> &gt; <strong>MOF XMI File</strong> &gt; <strong>MOF Selection</strong>.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the <strong>Select </strong><strong>Packages </strong><strong>to </strong><strong>Export</strong> dialog, select packages you want to export and click <strong>Add</strong> to move them to the <strong>Selected</strong> list. Click <strong>OK</strong> when you are done.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_packages_to_export_mof.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting projects to MOF files" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Perform the further steps as described in the previous <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">export </span></span>procedure (starting</span></span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> from <strong>step #2</strong>).</span></li></ol><p style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Exported </strong><strong>elements validation</strong></span><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> The modeling tool provides two validation suites (one for CMOF and one for EMOF) for validating models that are being exported to MOF files. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674385 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00476: Exporting table

- page_id: `169674385`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674385/Exporting+table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:40:18.739+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Basic tasks in tables
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

You can export the table to an *.html, *.csv, or *.xlsx file.

Before exporting a table, you can:

- Customize the template.html according to your needs. - The style of *.html tables is specified in template.html which can be found in <modeling tool installation directory>\data\table.- The file stores cascading style sheets (CSS) that define the appearance of an exported table.
- Prepare a table by showing only particular data.

To export a table

1. On the Publish toolbar, click the Export button.
2. In the opened dialog, do the following:
  1. Browse for a location to save the table.
  2. Type the exported table name.
  3. Select the exported table format.
3. Click **Save** when you are done. 
The table is exported to the location you have specified. Importing requirements from files that were exported using the[IMAGE alt='' src='']**Export**option creates new elements instead of importing the existing ones. To avoid that, make sure to use [IMAGE alt='' src='']**[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space='MD2024xR1']** instead.

To export table to Excel/CSV file and link it with modeling tool table

- On the table toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''], and select **Excel/CSV File** >**Write To File**. In the **Save** dialog, select the location from your file system, and save the new Excel or CSV file to your computer. 
The Excel or CSV file is exported, linked with the modeling tool table, and ready to be synced. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space='MD2024xR1']>]]> The file cannot be saved if a file with the same name and in the same location already exists.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673551 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00477: Exporting UML models

- page_id: `169673551`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673551/Exporting+UML+models
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:15:01.576+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects
- labels: ['exporting-uml', 'export-property-values', 'uml-export']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Modeling tool allows exporting projects to the following file formats:

- UML XMI 2.5 file. You can export a project to an .xmi file.
- MagicDraw Native XML file. You can export a project to an .xml file.
- EMF Ecore file. You can export a whole project or selected packages to an .ecore file.
- MOF XMI file. You can export a whole model or selected packages to a MOF (both CMOF and EMOF) XMI file.
- Eclipse UML2 (v2.x, v3.x, v4.x, v5.x) XMI file. You can export a project to an Eclipse based UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) compatible XMI file.

To export a project

1. On the main menu, click **File**>**Export To.**
2. Select a file format you want to export your project to.
3. The following actions depend on the dialog corresponding to the selected file format.

##### Exporting projects as Eclipse UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI files

Export a model to an Eclipse based UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) compatible XMI file enables the interchange of the UML2 models for further manipulations and transformations with the most popular MDA tools. You can also export your project as a template.

To export a project as an Eclipse UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI file

1. Open a project you want to export as an Eclipse UML2 (v1.x / v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI file.
2. On the File menu, click Export To and select one of the following commands:
  - Eclipse UML2 (v2.x) XMI file .
  - Eclipse UML2 (v3.x) XMI file .
  - Eclipse UML2 (v4.x) XMI file .
  - Eclipse UML2 (v5.x) XMI file .
3. Specify a location for exported project files.
4. Click Export .

- A project exported as an Eclipse UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI file is saved with the .uml file extension.

To change export property values

1. From the Options menu, select Environment . The Environment Options dialog opens.
2. Click the Eclipse UML2 XMI property group and select one of the following options groups:
  - Eclipse UML2 (v2.x) XMI file .
  - Eclipse UML2 (v3.x) XMI file .
  - Eclipse UML2 (v4.x) XMI file .
  - Eclipse UML2 (v5.x) XMI file .
3. In the property list, specify the desired property values. You can see descriptions of each property in the description area below the property list.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

You can also export your project as a template.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To export a project as an Eclipse UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI file</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a project you want to export as an Eclipse UML2 (v1.x / v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI file.</li><li>On the <strong>File</strong> menu, click <strong>Export To</strong> and select one of the following commands:<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span><ul><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v2.x) XMI file</strong>.</li><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v3.x) XMI file</strong>.</li><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v4.x) XMI file</strong>. <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v5.x) XMI file</strong>. <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li></ul></li><li>Specify a location for exported project files.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li><li>Click <strong>Export</strong>. <br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0965708-3236-4fec-b26e-a53d4e500093"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>A project exported as an Eclipse UML2 (v2.x / v3.x / v4.x / v5.x) XMI file is saved with the <em>.uml</em> file extension.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To change export property values</p><hr /><ol><li>From the <strong>Options</strong> menu, select <strong>Environment</strong>. The <strong>Environment Options</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>Click the <strong>Eclipse UML2 XMI </strong>property group and select one of the following options groups:<br /><ul><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v2.x) XMI file</strong>.</li><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v3.x) XMI file</strong>.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v4.x) XMI file</strong>.</li><li><strong>Eclipse UML2 (v5.x) XMI file</strong>. <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><br /></span></li></ul></li><li>In the property list, specify the desired property values. You can see descriptions of each property in the description area below the property list.</li></ol>

## PAGE 00478: Expression Evaluation

- page_id: `169676561`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676561/Expression+Evaluation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:22.426+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

69291813

69291825

69291815

The expression evaluation capability in the expression editor allows you to run/execute an expression on the actual testing model while editing.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Expression Evaluation dialog showing the expression body on the left and values of parameters on the right.

To open the **Expression Evaluation** dialog

- In the modeling tool main menu, click Tools, and select Expression Evaluation .

While evaluating your expressions, choose:

- **Language**. Select the language for the expression. To ensure the best performance experience, it is strongly suggested that you use the recommended languages (Binary, Structured Expression, or Groovy). The icon color next to the language selection reflects performance, i.e., green for fast (Binary, Structured Expression, Groovy), yellow for medium (Jython, OCL2.0), and red for slow (Javascript Rhino). If the performance is unknown, an info icon appears.
- Use Selection . Set to true , if you want to use the selected elements as the evaluation criteria. Elements can be selected either in the Model Browser or in the diagram, but not both.
- Iterate through selection . Select the check box to evaluate model elements one by one.

The Expression Evaluation is also available in the dialogs, wherein you can define expressions, e.g., **Expression** editor, **Dependency Criteria**, **Body and Language** dialogs. If you want to start using the expression evaluation in one of these dialogs, click the **Evaluation Mode** button.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Dependency Criteria dialog showing the location of the Evaluation Mode button on the right.

69291812

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
- Object Constraint Language
- Smart Package
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Scripting languages performance' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679226 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00479: Expressions merge

- page_id: `169679226`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679226/Expressions+merge
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:30.215+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1852110634

1852110637

1852110636

Suppose you have two customization elements extending the same UML element (for example, the same element specified as a customization target in both customization elements). Each customization element has a derived property. If the derived properties’ names, types, and multiplicities are the same, then the expressions defined for these derived properties are merged. Consequently, the extended UML element will have only one derived property instead of two and the property values will be calculated according to the union of expressions defined in both derived properties.

This occurs when the derived property (already specified in, for example, the UML Standard Profile) is also specified in a project. Note that in contrast to the example above the real-life expression merge can be performed in more complex situations with more than two derived properties having more than one expression defined. The expression merge can combine different types of expressions.

1852110633

**Related Pages**

- Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties
- Using Customization Data
- Creating Customization Data

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="05b88989-4cb1-4009-9c1a-b008bc772ac2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852110634</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d959b93-f142-4719-94c0-1c646dff57db"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852110637</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f6a00dea-335a-4033-bdee-fbef99967679"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852110636</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Suppose you have two customization elements extending the same UML element (for example, the same element specified as a customization target in both customization elements). Each customization element has a derived property. If the derived properties’ names, types, and multiplicities are the same, then the expressions defined for these derived properties are merged. Consequently, the extended UML element will have only one derived property instead of two and the property values will be calculated according to the union of expressions defined in both derived properties.</p><p>This occurs when the derived property (already specified in, for example, the UML Standard Profile) is also specified in a project. Note that in contrast to the example above the real-life expression merge can be performed in more complex situations with more than two derived properties having more than one expression defined. The expression merge can combine different types of expressions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="769562e9-b220-4b8c-92d0-be622953898d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852110633</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Extending+Metamodel+with+Derived+Properties">Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties</a></li><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+Customization+Data">Using Customization Data</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Creating+Customization+Data">Creating Customization Data</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675122 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00480: Extend

- page_id: `169675122`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675122/Extend
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:44:03.275+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Extend Use Case indicates how and when the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='MD2024xR1'] defined in the extending Use Case can be inserted into the behavior defined in the extended Use Case. The Extend relationship is a relationship for an extending Use Case to the extended Use Case. An Extend relationship can have a condition. If the condition is true, then the Extend [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space='MD2024xR1'] is performed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of Extend relationship

The extended Use Case owns one or more specific [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extension Point' space='MD2024xR1']. An extension point identifies a point in the behavior of a Use Case where that behavior can be extended.

In the preceding example, the*Penalize for Overdue*Use Case extends the *Register Item Return* Use Case. The *Register Item Return* Use Case owns the *Return Violation* extension point.

You can format the extend symbol properties in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='MD2024xR1']** dialog.

You can specify Extend properties in the Extend [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']. You can find the description of each property In the same window. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window.

#### TIP: Editing property values

Editing property values

For more information about specifying property values, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024xR1'].

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']

#### PANEL: Related Resources

Related Resources

Example of the Use Case diagram in*<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\diagrams\use case diagram.mdzip**.*

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679217 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00481: Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties

- page_id: `169679217`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679217/Extending+Metamodel+with+Derived+Properties
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:29.877+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1851478267

1851478269

1851478268

DSL customization allows extending the UML metamodel or its profile (such as SysML or UPDM) by creating new read-only derived properties.

A derived property's values are automatically calculated from the other properties’ values.

Derived properties are calculated on the fly. The modeling tool analyzes the derived properties and calculates them for existing model elements (when loading a model) and dynamically updates them according to model changes.

Derived properties can be added either to the standard UML elements, or to the ones extended with stereotypes. To extend the metamodel with the derived properties, there is no need to apply the stereotypes: the metamodel is extended in runtime.

Define derived properties using different expression types: Simple, Meta Chain, OCL, and Binary (reference to java code).

Easily access derived properties of a select model element, navigate to its specifications, or use the derived properties in model analysis tools (dependency matrices, report templates, and relation maps).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Derived properties specification metamodel.

1851478266

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and specifying derived properties' space='MD2024xR1']
- Defining expressions
- Recursive properties
- Expressions merge
- Derived properties visibility

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677101 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00482: Extending sequence diagrams

- page_id: `169677101`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677101/Extending+sequence+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-04-11T11:18:44.849+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Model Visualizer > Sequence Diagram from Java Source Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

640593856

640593858

640593857

Creating a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD190'] from a Java code allows you to analyze dependencies between the methods represented on the diagram and the classes they are referencing to and/or the other methods they call for. This means you can create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD190'] for any method selected in the existing [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD190'].

To create a new sequence diagram for a selected method

1. Select the method (call message with an operation assigned) whose details you want to see.
2. Right-click the message and, from the shortcut menu, select **Reverse Implementation**. The **Sequence Diagram from Java Source Wizard** opens to create a sequence diagram for the selected method. If you have started the wizard via the operation assigned to the message, be aware that in this case the first step of the wizard will be skipped, and the wizard will start from Step #2.

All selected method dependencies will be represented in a newly created diagram.

640593854

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='MD2024xR1']

This means you can create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD190" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[sequence diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for any method selected in the existing <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD190" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[sequence diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a new sequence diagram for a selected method</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the method (call message with an operation assigned) whose details you want to see.</li><li><p>Right-click the message and, from the shortcut menu, select <strong>Reverse Implementation</strong>. The <strong>Sequence Diagram from Java Source Wizard</strong> opens to create a sequence diagram for the selected method.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08fa7d29-4243-458f-b163-8bbc8666f2ae"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you have started the wizard via the operation assigned to the message, be aware that in this case the first step of the wizard will be skipped, and the wizard will start from Step #2.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p>All selected method dependencies will be represented in a newly created diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6db49fc9-04a1-45b4-9d02-6056201dcdc3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">640593854</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675125 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00483: Extension

- page_id: `169675125`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675125/Extension
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:04.866+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Extension is used to indicate that the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Properties' space='MD2024xR1'] of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metaclass' space='MD2024xR1'] are extended through a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024xR1'], and provides the flexibility to add (and later remove) stereotypes to a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024xR1'].

An Extension is a kind of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024xR1']. One end of the Extension is an ordinary Property that ties the Extension to a Class. The other end is an ExtensionEnd tying the Extension to a stereotype that extends the Class.

You can specify an Extension by changing its property values in the Extension[Specification window](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Specification+window). Each property is described in the description area on this window.

You can format the Extension symbol properties in the Symbol Properties dialog.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Extension is used to indicate that the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Properties" /></ac:link> of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Metaclass" /></ac:link> are extended through a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Stereotype" /></ac:link>, and provides the flexibility to add (and later remove) stereotypes to a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class" /></ac:link>.</p><p>An Extension is a kind of <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Association" /></ac:link>. One end of the Extension is an ordinary Property that ties the Extension to a Class. The other end is an ExtensionEnd tying the Extension to a stereotype that extends the Class.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You can specify an Extension by changing its property values in the Extension </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Specification+window">Specification window</a><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. Each property is described in the description area on this window.</span>  </p><p>You can format the Extension symbol properties in the <a>Symbol Properties dialog</a>.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675496 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00485: Extension Point

- page_id: `169675496`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675496/Extension+Point
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T13:22:02.020+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Use Case
- labels: ['use-case-extension-points']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

19692200

19692202

19692201

INLINE

An extension point in the Use Case represents the location at which the extension can be inserted. In the Use Case diagram, the extension point is used together with the extend relationship.

To add an extension point to the Use Case

1. Draw an extend relationship from the extending Use Case to the extended Use Case.
2. Subsequent actions depend on whether the extended Use Case has at least one extension point already:
  - If there are no extension points, a dialog appears asking for confirmation to add a new extension point. Click Yes and type the name of the extension point.
  - If there is at least one extension point, the Extension Points dialog opens, displaying the list of extension points of that Use Case. Click Create , type the name of the new extension point, and click Close .

You can also add extension points via the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'] of the Use Case. To do this, click **Extension Point**> [IMAGE alt='' src=''], and then type the name of the new extension point.

19692199

**Related pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024xR1']

In the Use Case diagram, the extension point is used together with the extend relationship.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add an extension point to the Use Case</p><hr /><ol><li>Draw an extend relationship from the extending Use Case to the extended Use Case.</li><li>Subsequent actions depend on whether the extended Use Case has at least one extension point already:<ul><li>If there are no extension points, a dialog appears asking for confirmation to add a new extension point. Click <strong>Yes</strong> and type the name of the extension point.</li><li>If there is at least one extension point, the <strong>Extension Points</strong> dialog opens, displaying the list of extension points of that Use Case. Click <strong>Create</strong>, type the name of the new extension point, and click <strong>Close</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ul></li></ol><p>You can also add extension points via the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of the Use Case. To do this, click <strong>Extension Point </strong>&gt; <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Extension Point" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>, and then type the name of the new extension point. </p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1acb222f-4a34-46f8-95e2-8fecd9f1310d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">19692199</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f0c21b04-53a5-482c-9cb6-957e09ac89e3"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Use Case" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675723 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00486: Extract Activity Wizard

- page_id: `169675723`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675723/Extract+Activity+Wizard
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:15.799+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Extracting > Extracting in Activity diagram
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

263986023

263986034

263986022

**On this page**

****

263986024

The**Extract Activity Wizard** is used for extracting a selected part of an activity diagram to a separate diagram.

Using this wizard, you can:

- Create and specify the target diagram that will be created as the result of the extraction. The selected part will be extracted to the newly created diagram.
- Select the parameters that will be created in a new diagram. The parameters are listed for each intersected object flow from the source diagram.
- Specify a referencing element that is created in the source activity diagram and represents the elements moved to the target diagram during the extraction.

To start the **Extract Activity Wizard**

1. In the activity diagram, select a part that you want to extract.
2. From the selected part’s shortcut menu, choose Refactor > Extract . The Extract Activity Wizard opens.

This section describes components of the **Extract Activity Wizard**, such as text boxes and buttons.

The **Extract Activity Wizard** consists of three steps:

1. Specification of a new element
2. Creation of parameters
3. Creation of a reference element

###### Step #1. Specification of a new diagram

In this step, you can specify a diagram into which the extracted part will be moved and a package wherein this new diagram will be created. You can also define the type of a newly created diagram here. The activity diagram type is selected by default.

The new diagram will be created along with a new activity - the diagram will be created as an inner activity element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

| Element name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Type diagram name | Type the name of the new diagram wherein the extracted part will be stored. |
| Select owner | In the model tree, select a package, profile, or model as the owner for the newly created activity. You can also:Create a new owner. Click the Create Owner button, select an element type, and specify its properties.Clone an existing package, profile, or model. In the model tree, select the owner, click the Clone button, and specify properties of the cloned element. |
| Select diagram type | Open the list and select the diagram type for the extracted part. The default diagram type is activity diagram. You can also select to create an interaction overview diagram. By using the customized plugins, you can add custom diagrams to the diagram type list. |

After you have specified the new diagram name, owner of the diagram, and the type of the new diagram, do one of the following:

- Click Next , if you want to specify parameters and to define a referencing element. The next wizard step opens.
- Click Finish . The selected part of an activity diagram is extracted to a new diagram that is specified in this step. Instead of the extracted part, the Call Behavior Action is created in the activity diagram referencing to the newly created diagram. The second and the third wizard steps are skipped - that is the default options are taken.

###### Step #2. Selection of parameters

The parameter(s) that will be created for the intersected object flow is listed in this step. Along with the parameter(s) in the newly created target diagram, the activity parameter node will be created. The activity parameter node has the pin created as an inner element, as well as the assigned parameter.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- The Select parameters list is empty unless there are intersected object flows selected in the source activity diagram.
- For more information about intersected object flows, see concepts in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extracting' space='MD2024xR1'] .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Step #3. Specification of a referencing element

A referencing element is created in the source diagram instead of the extracted part. Double-click the referencing element to navigate to the extracted part.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

| Element name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Type element name | Type the name of the referencing element that will be created in the source activity diagram in place of the selected elements. |
| Select element type | Open the list and select the referencing element type. The default referencing element type is a call behavior action. Custom elements can be added to the referencing element type list using customized plugins. |

After you have selected the parameters, do one of the following:

- Click Back to return to the first step.
- Click Finish . The selected part of an activity diagram is extracted to a new diagram.

263991999

**Related pages**

- Extracting
- Extracting in Activity diagram
- Extraction rules in Activity diagram
- Working with model elements

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675705 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00487: Extract Sequence Wizard

- page_id: `169675705`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675705/Extract+Sequence+Wizard
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:15.378+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Extracting > Extracting in Sequence diagram
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

257629919

257629930

257629918

**On this page**

257629920

The **Extract Sequence Wizard** is used for extracting a selected part of the sequence diagram to a separate diagram. Using this wizard, you can:

- Create and specify the new element wherein the extracted part will be moved.
- Select the intersected incoming messages for which the formal gates will be created in the target diagram.

To start the **Extract Sequence Wizard**

1. On a sequence diagram, select a part that you want to extract.
2. From the selected part’s shortcut menu, select Refactor > Extract . The Extract Sequence Wizard opens.

The **Extract Sequence Wizard** consists of two steps:

1. Specification of a new element
2. Selection of messages

###### Step #1. Specification of a new element

In this step, you can specify a diagram into which the extracted part will be moved and an owner wherein this new diagram will be created. You can also define the type of the newly created element that will own the diagram. The interaction element and the sequence diagram type are selected by default. The name of the sequence diagram will be the same as the specified name of the interaction element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

| Element name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Type interaction name | Type the name of the new interaction, wherein the extracted part will be stored and the new sequence diagram will be created. The newly created sequence diagram name will be the same as the interaction name. |
| Select element type | Open the list and select the element type in which the extracted part and new diagram will be created. The default element type is interaction.By using the customization plugin, you can add custom elements to the element type list. |
| Select owner | In the model tree, select a package, profile, or model as an owner of the newly created element. You can also:Create a new owner. Click the Create Owner button, select an element type, and specify its properties.Clone an existing package, profile, or model. To clone, in the model tree, select an owner, click the Clone button, and specify properties of the cloned element. |
| Select diagram type | Open the list and select the diagram type for the extracted part. The default diagram type is a sequence diagram. You can also select to create a new time diagram.By using the customized plugins, you can add custom diagrams to the diagram type list. |

After you have specified the new element name, type, owner, and the type of the new diagram, do one of the following:

- Click Next , if you want to select intersected messages that will be created in the new target diagram.
- Click Finish . The selected part of a sequence diagram is extracted to a new diagram. The second wizard step is skipped, and the messages that by default are selected in this step, will be created.

###### Step #2. Selection of messages

The incoming intersected messages selected in the source diagram are listed in this step. Select the messages that will be extracted to the new target diagram. The formal gates will be created for the selected messages.

- The Select messages list is empty unless there are intersected incoming messages that are selected in the source sequence diagram.
- If there are intersected outgoing replay messages when they are listed in the Extract Sequence Wizard , the second step. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rules of extracting in Sequence diagram' space='MD2024xR1'] .
- For more information about intersected messages, see concepts in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extracting' space='MD2024xR1'] .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Click**Back**to return to the first step.After you have selected the messages, do one of the following:

- Click Finish . The selected part of the sequence diagram is extracted to a new diagram.

257634635

**Related pages**

- Extracting
- Extracting in Sequence diagram
- Rules of extracting in Sequence diagram
- Working with model elements

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2385fe63-0955-463c-bb24-2e47504a3fba"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">257629919</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0eddb1fb-5739-43a8-9b84-832567f334cd"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">257629930</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dabc3287-3030-433a-b7c7-68a367e6b291"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">257629918</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>On this page</strong></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="536eb971-4655-44a6-b218-aa3992cbef8e" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11c14ff4-d90a-497e-8f05-9531174d3907"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">257629920</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>Extract Sequence Wizard</strong> is used for extracting a selected part of the sequence diagram to a separate diagram. The <strong>Extract Sequence Wizard</strong> opens.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>The <strong>Extract Sequence Wizard</strong> consists of two steps:</p><ol><li>Specification of a new element</li><li>Selection of messages</li></ol><h4>Step #1. Specification of a new element</h4><p>In this step, you can specify a diagram into which the extracted part will be moved and an owner wherein this new diagram will be created. You can also define the type of the newly created element that will own the diagram. The interaction element and the sequence diagram type are selected by default. The name of the sequence diagram will be the same as the specified name of the interaction element.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="extract_sequence_wizard_1st_step.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Extract Sequence Wizard" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Element name</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td>Type interaction name</td><td><p>Type the name of the new interaction, wherein the extracted part will be stored and the new sequence diagram will be created. The newly created sequence diagram name will be the same as the interaction name.</p></td></tr><tr><td>Select element type</td><td><p>Open the list and select the element type in which the extracted part and new diagram will be created. The default element type is interaction.</p><p>By using the customization plugin, you can add custom elements to the element type list.</p></td></tr><tr><td>Select owner</td><td><p>In the model tree, select a package, profile, or model as an owner of the newly created element. You can also:</p><ul><li>Create a new owner. Click the <strong>Create Owner</strong> button, select an element type, and specify its properties.</li><li>Clone an existing package, profile, or model. To clone, in the model tree, select an owner, click the <strong>Clone</strong> button, and specify properties of the cloned element.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">Select diagram type</td><td colspan="1"><p>Open the list and select the diagram type for the extracted part. The default diagram type is a sequence diagram. You can also select to create a new time diagram.</p><p>By using the customized plugins, you can add custom diagrams to the diagram type list.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p>After you have specified the new element name, type, owner, and the type of the new diagram, do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Click <strong>Next</strong>, if you want to select intersected messages that will be created in the new target diagram.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Finish</strong>. The selected part of a sequence diagram is extracted to a new diagram. The second wizard step is skipped, and the messages that by default are selected in this step, will be created.</li></ul><h4>Step #2. Selection of messages</h4><p>The incoming intersected messages selected in the source diagram are listed in this step. Select the messages that will be extracted to the new target diagram. The formal gates will be created for the selected messages.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3ba6e538-3005-466d-b099-fbb07190f98d"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>The <strong>Select messages</strong> list is empty unless there are intersected incoming messages that are selected in the source sequence diagram.</li><li>If there are intersected outgoing replay messages when they are listed in the <strong>Extract Sequence Wizard</strong>, the second step. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Rules of extracting in Sequence diagram" /></ac:link>.</li><li>For more information about intersected messages, see concepts in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Extracting" /></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:title="Extract Sequence Wizard. Select messages" ac:alt="Extract Sequence Wizard. Select messages"><ri:attachment ri:filename="extracting_sequence5.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Extract Sequence Wizard" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><span style="line-height: 1.42857;"><br />Click </span><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Back</strong><span style="line-height: 1.42857;"> to return to the first step. </span>After you have selected the messages, do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Click <strong>Finish</strong>. The selected part of the sequence diagram is extracted to a new diagram.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5cf14db3-d9a5-4afc-8cb0-3a66c4594b40"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">257634635</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Extracting">Extracting</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Extracting+in+Sequence+diagram">Extracting in Sequence diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Rules+of+extracting+in+Sequence+diagram">Rules of extracting in Sequence diagram</a></li><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Working+with+model+elements">Working with model elements</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675732 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00488: Extract State Machine Wizard

- page_id: `169675732`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675732/Extract+State+Machine+Wizard
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:16.026+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Extracting > Extracting in State Machine diagram
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

264738486

264738497

264738484

**On this page**

264738487

The**Extract State Machine Wizard** is used for extracting a selected part of a state machine diagram to a separate diagram. Using this wizard, you can:

- Create and specify the target diagram that will be created as the result of the extraction. The selected part will be extracted to the newly created diagram.
- Select the entry and exit points that will be created in a new diagram. The entry and exit points are listed for each intersected transition from the source diagram.
- Specify a referencing element that is created in the source state machine diagram and represents the elements moved to the target diagram during the extraction.

To start the **Extract State Machine Wizard**

1. In the state machine diagram, select a part that you want to extract.
2. From the selected part’s shortcut menu, choose Refactor > Extract . The Extract State Machine Wizard opens.

The **Extract State Machine Wizard** consists of three steps:

1. Specification of a new element
2. Creation of entry/exit points
3. Specification of a submachine

###### Step #1. Specification of a new element

In this step, you can specify a diagram into which the extracted part will be moved. The new diagram will be created along with a new state machine. The diagram will be created as an inner state machine element. You can also define the element type, name, and the owner. Note, that the diagram name will be the same as the element name. The state machine type is selected by default.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

| Element name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Type element name | Type the name of the new element wherein the extracted part will be stored. The new element will contain the new diagram. |
| Select element type | Open the list and select the element type in which the extracted part and new diagram will be created. The default element type is a state machine. By using customized plugins, you can add custom elements to the element type list. |
| Select owner | In the model tree, select a package, profile, or model as an owner of the newly created state machine. You can also:Create a new owner. Click the Create Owner button, select an element type, and specify its properties.Clone an existing package, profile, or model. To clone, in the model tree, select an owner, click the Clone button, and specify properties of the cloned element. |
| Select diagram type | Open the list and select the diagram type for the extracted part. The default diagram type is a state machine diagram. By using customized plugins, you can add custom diagrams to the diagram type list. |

After you have specified the new element name, an owner of the element, and the type of the new diagram, do one of the following:

- Click Next , if you want to specify entry/exit points and define a referencing element. The next wizard step opens.
- Click Finish . The selected part of a state machine diagram is extracted to a new diagram that is specified in this step. Instead of the extracted part, the submachine state is created referencing to the newly created diagram. The second and the third wizard steps are skipped - that is the default options are taken.

###### Step #2. Creation of entry/exit points

The entry and exit points that will be created for the intersected transitions are listed in this step.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example, in the *Access Control* source diagram, the intersected transitions are selected for the extraction. After the extraction, in the target diagram, the entry and exit points are created for each intersected transition.

In the **Extract State Machine Wizard**, the **Create entry/exit point** step is empty unless there are intersected transitions selected in the source state machine diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

After you have selected the parameters, do one of the following:

- Click Back to return to the first step.
- Click Next to define a referencing element. The next wizard step opens.
- Click Finish . The selected part of the state machine diagram is extracted to a new diagram. The third wizard step is skipped - that is the default submachine name is taken.

###### Step #3. Specification of a submachine

A referencing element is created in the source diagram instead of the extracted part. Double-click the referencing element to navigate to the extracted part.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

After you have typed the submachine name, do one of the following:

- Click Back to return to the second step.
- Click Finish . The selected part of the state machine diagram is extracted to the new state machine diagram.

264760283

**Related pages**

- Extracting
- Extracting in State Machine diagram
- Working with model elements
